content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901         322-6441         322-6444

January 21, 1980

Mr. Donald Pollard
Assistant City Manager
100 N. Garfield Avenue
Pasadena, CA 91109

Dear Mr. Pollard:

Thank you for your letter dated January 4, 1980 seeking
advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act pursuant to Government Code Section
83114(b). The facts as I understand them are as follows.

You presently hold the position of Assistant City
Manager in charge of matters pertaining to economic development; as such, you are actively involved in government consideration of all major construction projects and other development programs that take place within the city. It is your intention to resign your office and to do private consultant work in Pasadena. You have not decided whether to work independently or in association with development companies.

The question you have asked the Fair Political Practices Commission to consider is whether it is a violation of the Political Reform Act for you to do private consultant work in Pasadena and represent clients before the city after resigning your official position. If not, is it a violation if the city hires you as a consultant on a part-time basis. Finally, you ask if it is permissible for you to do private consultant work prior to your resignation so long as you refrain from participating in decisions that might affect your private employer.

City of Pasadena
ONE HUNDRED NORTH GARFIELD AVENUE

PASADENA, CALIFORNIA 91109

THE OFFICE OF CITY MANAGER

January 4, 1980


Mr. Dwight Dickerson
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Dear Mr. Dickerson:

Request for Advice

     Thank you for the opportunity to discuss the provisions of
the Political Reform Act with you this morning on the telephone.
This letter is a request for advice regarding my anticipated depart-
ure from City service.

     As I indicated to you, I have held the position of Assistant
City Manager for the City of Pasadena for the past eleven years.
The primary function of that office is economic development. In
that capacity I deal with nearly all of the developers and major
capital programs in the city. Approximately one year ago, I publicly
indicated to the City Manager and the City Board of Directors that
I intended to resign and to leave the state.

     Recently, I have become interested in remaining in the field
of development and I am considering becoming a consultant - either
independently or in association with another development company.

     At this point in time I have not accepted any offer of em-
ployment, nor have I discussed terms of any consulting arrangement.
It is obvious, however, that because of my acquaintances I may want
to work in a consulting capacity on projects in Pasadena.  It is
highly unlikely that if I were to work on projects in Pasadena, that
they would not be projects which have been discussed in one form or
another prior to this time.  For that reason, I have the following
questions:

     1.  May I become an independent consultant or associate
with a private development company at any time after I have resigned
my position?

     2.  After I have resigned, is it possible for me to work
on a consulting basis for the City of Pasadena?

Mr. Dwight Dickerson                    - 2 -                    January 4, 1980


     3.  If I become involved with a project on behalf of a
developer involving a project in the City of Pasadena, may I represent that developer before the City Board or in transactions with
the City?

     4.  Is my understanding correct that if prior to my departure
from City employment I agree to become a consultant with a developer
whose project is located in Pasadena, that I have not violated the
Political Reform Act, so long as I refrain from participating in
any discussion of that project while I am employed?

     I believe these questions cover the area of our discussion.
I would appreciate a response from you relating to these questions
and addressing any further points which you may feel need to be
addressed as a result of our telephone conversation.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Donald L. Pollard
                                        Assistant City Manager

P:S

City of Pasadena
ONE HUNDRED NORTH GARFIELD AVENUE

PASADENA, CALIFORNIA 91109

January 23, 1980


Mr. Dwight E. Dickerson
Legal Counsel, Legal Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Dickerson:

     Thank you very much for your prompt response
to my inquiry.  You phrased the questions in a very
helpful manner - both for me and for the members of
our City Council.

     Again, thanks for your assistance.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Donald L. Pollard
                                        Assistant City Manager

P:S

sections.question_synthetic: An Assistant City Manager in Pasadena seeks advice on post-employment restrictions under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding whether he can work as a development consultant after resignation, including whether he can represent developers on projects in Pasadena and whether he can agree to such arrangements before his departure.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is the incoming request letter, not the FPPC response. A follow-up thank-you letter from January 23, 1980 is included but contains no new substantive questions or FPPC conclusions. The actual FPPC advice letter (80A063) is not included in this document.

embedding.summary: This is a two-part document: an incoming request for FPPC advice from an Assistant City Manager planning to resign and transition to development consulting, followed by a brief thank-you letter acknowledging FPPC's response. The requestor seeks clarification on post-employment restrictions and conflict of interest issues.
